interactions.summary: The host compares American disappointment to late Soviet stagnation and suggests failed utopian desire may be hollowing out everyday life.
Jiang answers with the Houthis as a counterexample, arguing that purpose, sincerity, and willingness to die make poor but committed people happier than decadent Westerners.
The host pivots to the Middle East and asks how current bloodshed can be understood through biblical story and theology rather than only through oil and chaos.
Jiang says Jerusalem and the Levant matter both as sacred geography and as the ancient center of trade, which is why the region keeps driving world history.

claims.claim: Jiang uses the Houthis in Yemen as an example of people who may be materially poor but are happier because they have purpose, sincerity, and a cause larger than themselves.
Jiang says Western society fears death so intensely that it strips life of risk, sacrifice, and therefore meaning.
Jiang argues that the Levant has been central to history not only for theological reasons but because it was the crossroads of trade linking Egypt, Anatolia, Mesopotamia, and Europe.
Jiang describes Jerusalem as an unusually sacred city whose felt holiness helps explain why people believe history both began there and will end there.

signature_moments.moment: War and death are said to give meaning, while safe Western life drains it away.
signature_moments.source_phrase: War and death gives meaning to people.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is one of Jiang's sharpest reversals of modern liberal common sense.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang treats Jerusalem as a city whose holiness can be physically felt.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You can feel all that holy energy.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains why he treats sacred geography as an active historical force rather than mere symbolism.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The trade and sacred corridor Jiang treats as the long-term epicenter of global historical struggle.
